I guess my first question I wanted to just kind of ask is, is that the right way of even thinking about this process? Um, it's the question: how do customers happen? So if that's how customers happen, then yes. Okay, yeah, absolutely. Can you tell me a little bit about your business? Hmm.
Um, so we help—beginners, essentially— start and grow businesses online, primarily through— sort of like affiliate marketing and then into product creation, course creation, things like this. Okay. So you have a Facebook ad. And then they land on your sales page. They opt in. And then you retarget them. Or they land on your order form. They complete the order. They don't complete the order that you follow up for the added cart, abandoned cart. Um. You also offer. Lay it on thank you page. And then you go, you drop into your fulfillment engine. Now, do you see any issues with, are you having any issues with conversion with your growth engine at all? Um, not currently. Okay. Um, then yeah, I mean, if this is how growth is happening, then.
Simple doesn't necessarily mean bad. If it's not broke, don't fix it. If you're looking to optimize it. I mean, I wouldn't change anything about your growth engine if it's working. Only thing I would do is if you're seeing leaky, like stuff leaking out of your funnel, like for example. If you're seeing people land on your sales page. And then they opt in. If they don't opt in. So maybe. Test is separate, a second opt-in for the people who don't. Opt-in, potentially? Instead of retargeting them back to the same page, maybe retarget, maybe test another opt-in to see if the people who didn't opt in. We'll be open to trying a different type of opt-in. Maybe it's positioned a little bit differently. I would only optimize for the stragglers and not optimize for people who are actually going through.
Unless there's like a time issue, where, let's say, you're getting the opt-in, but their time to complete is like 100 days if you want to get that down to like 30. Then maybe you could do some optimization there. Bye. If it's working, I wouldn't necessarily mess with it. I would just look at where you're having people fall out. And find ways to capture more of those people. Okay, yeah, I got you. So is the perspective really when you're doing the growth engine process and you've got these, is it really that you're kind of using these as points of reference to be able to improve upon them, like if you need to? Is that what kind of really we're referring back to these for? Yeah.
And then you want to be able to build out a growth scorecard so that you can identify when things are slowly dipping into the yellow and the red. Too often marketers will not notice that things are dipping. Are they looking at the wrong metrics? Really? They don't have. Um. They don't have KPIs for every step of the journey. Completing the order. Um... Is there a customer service? stuff that needs to happen. I know you said it's largely automated, but if there are things that aren't. or there's things that are touch points that you need to have with those folks. to improve the excitement of your customers. I would document and notate those as well. um in those power stages because those are where you know the customer is agreeing to talk to you all. So I would imagine there's some degree of something that happens, even if it's automated. Funnels don't last forever. They change.
They adjust. So document those key areas, those key touch points where clients are saying yes. And identify what's supposed to happen in those areas because, when your growth engine changes, you're going to want to maintain that level of Um. engagement with them. Sure. And that's usually the first thing that goes away is the level of engagement and those touches. Right. Yeah, all right, perfect. No, that's great. Thank you. Amara, I just, I also saw, I know that you responded to Melanie. in the community. But I also just wanted to take this opportunity really quickly just to touch on that as well.
Maybe you can expand on some of your thoughts. Thoughts about Notion versus ClickUp. And maybe we can kind of circle back to a discussion that we had months ago about choosing softwares. and how to make that decision. Sure. So Melanie had asked about ClickUp and Notion and things like that. So tools are tools. It all depends on how you use them. So Notion's a great tool. I've used Notion for... probably eight years, really long time before ClickUp was even a thought. And so I've used both extensively. I really like Notion. It has some limitations. As with anything. It was supposed to be a note-taking tool. It wasn't necessarily supposed to be a project management tool. It wasn't supposed to be the big thing that Airtable is.
If you've ever heard of Airtable, it's kind of like. It's kind of like Excel on like. Massive amounts of speed. It's like. It's like three-dimensional. Like Excel tables. It's like crazy, crazy amounts of. Functionality and you can use it to like plan calendars. You can do all kinds of stuff with Airtable. So Notion's kind of like... you know, a version of Airtable and then a version of like. A note-taking app. Um, It's great to use. To help kind of keep yourself organized, it might be really really hard if you have an organization of five or more people to kind of keep things together because you need like notifications. There's certain functionalities that you need a little bit more of. She'd asked about ClickUp. ClickUp is a project management tool.
I love ClickUp, but... This whole 'one tool to rule them all' is very challenging. Because you know, tools are built to solve a problem, and so CRMs, customer relationship management software. They're made to help you foster relationships with your customers. They're made to help you understand. What, um... like where your customers are at, where your sales opportunities are at. Um. And if you use... You're contacts in marketing that you're getting from leads and stuff like that, and you put them in sales and do proper handoff and stuff, you can actually improve your conversions. ClickUp is not that, so it's not a CRM. It can be used as a database, but its biggest superpower is a project management tool.
And so whenever you're using any kind of software at all, Really think about What is the purpose of the software? Don't get sucked into what they're selling you that it can do because the software will never solve your problems. It will be. The problem that you have. Make sure that it can actually solve the problem that you have versus just saying it can do everything. So I would steer clear from that. And some people do make it work, but... Here's what happens with that. When you get really caught up in the techie part of it. APIs change. All sorts of different things change. And so one little change could ruin your whole workflow. And so I don't trust it a lot of times. So yes, you can make certain software work.
Melanie was talking about people using ClickUp as a CRM, people using Notion for other things. You can sometimes make it work, but. A lot of times those automations and stuff fail. And so your data is not always good. And if you don't, if you can't trust your data, a lot of people won't use it. So there's that. So hopefully that answered the question. Yeah. Yeah, no, I appreciate it. I think I just wanted to expand a little bit on, I know that you're thinking regarding. Software and choice of software is really insightful. Um so I just wanted to share that. Oh, actually, look at that—Melanie's popping on right now. That's funny. Oh, there she is. Perfect. That's actually really funny. Uh, I was just about to comment and let her know.
Melanie, uh, your timing is impeccable. Uh, I had just asked Amara to expand on her answer to you from the community, and I was like, literally, about to tag you—uh, and just let you know that we had discussed it some. Uh, so that you could drop in and watch the recording. Uh, and like, as I was about to type that, you uh started to log on. So it is, it is good to see you. Welcome. Thank you. Awesome. Are you planning to use ClickUp for project management or were you just thinking about using it for like dumping? data. Yeah, so we use ClickUp for our project management already. And I just talked to another agency owner and he showed me how he has his entire sales pipeline in ClickUp. Mm-hmm.
It was basically a ClickUp on steroids. Connected to his email, and would come in and he can mark it. And I was like, oh. Be really cool if we can centralize our platforms more, because right now we use Active Campaign and that connects to other platforms. So. I was wondering if I could take everything you guys do in Notion so well and somehow figure out how to put it in ClickUp because I love the way you guys put it in Notion. We're just kind of trying to ramp up our project management or our ClickUp software is kind of like the hub for everything. Hmm-hmm. I was just saying that I'm a huge fan of it, and there's so many debates on this in the ClickUp community, so you can take it with a grain of salt.
vehemently opposed to using ClickUp as a CRM. Just because it's... One, their API is is a little bit. How do I say this without getting in trouble? Um, it can have its failures and when that happens, there aren't notifications. There's not a whole lot of notifications in place for when the API doesn't work the way it's supposed to. And so it can be incredibly frustrating. I don't know how big your organization is, but. A CRM is a beautiful thing. And if you use a CRM the way it's supposed to be used, what you would have to do to click up to make it function as a CRM, the way a CRM truly should function, it's just too much. You would have to really rig it. Quite a bit.
I mean, yeah, some people do it. They get Integromat and get into the API and do all this stuff. But you could literally just do all that stuff out of the box with active campaign in HubSpot and not have to deal with You know, all the. The reading of it all. Some people love that stuff. I'm not a fan because. You know, one thing breaks and it just becomes kind of a mess. That's why I've been asking because I was like, well, when? You know, it's the fine line between centralizing everything you do in one platform and using every platform to its best of its abilities so that's why I was like, I'm sure these guys have have an idea on what the best way to go forward is.
And ClickUp is such a beast too. There are so many layers and so much you can do. So it's hard for me to be like. Is this the best way or not? So I appreciate the feedback. I personally, so I have a mastermind for agencies using ClickUp, and I don't use all the features for a reason. I don't, and again, there's so many schools of thought on it. I use the minimum amount of features for that reason. Just because. They're still building a lot of those and it can be really frustrating to try to like. build everything around this really cool feature and then have it break, or have it stop working, or have it. And so, like, I don't really use all the automations as much.
And that one for project management purposes, just because we don't really need them all. And we use some, just not. We're not like automation freaks, you know, with ClickUp because it just leads to too much. Too much. Uh, inconsistency, I guess. I would say. That doesn't mean you shouldn't just test, just test it out and see. No, I appreciate that. Just a heads up— so the tools that we use most primarily, that's the primary tools that we use here. Um, are Notion, obviously, and then our team uses Airtable. So the programs team, other teams might use other tools for project management, but we use Airtable for project management just because we we like the databases that we can then link with, like a project board. So we use that for more of the moving parts.
And then we also do a lot of collaboration in Google Docs. So I would say that those three are pretty much our TechStack. A lot more of our collaborative work and our collaborative thinking happens in Google Docs. I mean, you can do it in Notion. Notion is actually great for that with the commenting features. I think it's... just kind of Google Docs has been around for a while and we're just comfortable in it. But I mean, again, we use Notion primarily almost as like a company wiki where we really just. Like dropping all of the knowledge about our company and tracking, but a lot of our collaboration and project management happens in other tools that we feel are a bit better suited. But again, you can make Notion work for that too.
I mean, they can do. I believe someone else in the cohort is using it with a Kanban board. I mean, you can do it there as well. But I like that we can go to Notion when there's something that I need to look up as far as, you know, an SOP. I know it's in Notion. I know it's there. And it almost helps us like, at least helps me, kind of bifurcate my thinking where I'm like, 'I know that that's where I go for that.' You know, Google Doc is where our collaboration happens. And I think we're really using the best parts of the tools, rather than like, 'Oh, you can do it in Notion, but it's like I don't necessarily think that Notion is as robust for data and project management as a tool like Airtable.' Well, that's how I feel about ClickUp too, right?
Like, you can put documents and SOPs in there. But I know that I feel like Notion does that so much better. Again, it's like the fine line of: Do I put SOPs in ClickUp and it's like a little bit of a worse experience, but I have everything in one platform? Or do I really? And I kind of like the point you brought up, like it's nice to have One platform for one specific thing as well. Thank you. So yeah, I get to, I get to think about that more, but I appreciate the feedback. I think this was super helpful. Awesome. I'm glad. Cool. Yeah. And just keep us in the loop. Let us know. I mean, we're here to help. We're here to support obviously people in the cohort, our coaches, but also just people in the community and no shade.

They really are. But that's what I might do there, way too much. And again, August is a good friend. Um, Interesting stuff, though, for sure. It's interesting to me just to see how people are building different systems. I think almost too complex to maintain for me. I'm like, 'Give me the bear, like, minimum MVS. Right. Give me the minimum viable system. You know, make it easy. Make it easy to maintain. That's how I know I'm going to use it. That's how I know our teams are going to use it. If it's too complex, too crazy, gonna happen. Um. And that's where you're hitting it on the head. It's like, if something breaks, you're the only one that knows how to fix it because you built it. That's become... And that's what will end up happening with an August Bradley system. It's way too much. Um. Yeah, I'm actually reading Anti-Fragile right now.
It's what we're reading for our book club. And it's already got me thinking about that, where it's like his systems seem to be— robust or fragile, where you're like, again, with like what Amar is saying, if you're the only person that knows how to build it and you're the only person that's willing to, then it's not going to work. Whereas something that is simple and where you're using the best parts of the tools is going to be a. know an anti-fragile system— a robust system that people are going to want to use and you know find it easier to maintain and scale. Yeah, absolutely. And for what it's worth, I know a lot of people that use the ClickUp Docs. For and I'm going to move on to the next question.
Sorry, but a lot of people use the ClickUp Docs for SOPs. Um. It should be fine. You can totally do that as a knowledge base. Um, they have nesting and threading of docs and stuff like that so it can make it a lot easier. To kind of keep different categories of docs in one place. So you don't have a hundred docs strewn all over. Click Up, just create your own internal space and make sure that you keep them there. Have you considered moving that to the, maybe that'd be the first question so someone can waste their time doing the whole quiz? It's funny you mentioned that. Someone else mentioned that to me the other day as well about considering putting that in the beginning. I think it's a great idea, honestly. Because, I mean, they're going to get all the way to this, like, fifth screen and it's like my device isn't even power on.
And so. If you guys won't buy it at all, then... I mean, unless you guys recycle or do something else with it, then. I would just ask, I would just ask that question first. That way they can move on with their life and go do what they got to do. Okay. Got it. Thank you. And then do they have to remove their screen protectors and cases to answer those questions? So they, I mean, you don't necessarily need to, but an issue we run into a lot of the times is we get a phone in. From an operational standpoint, we remove the screen protector, and there's a crack under the screen protector that could not be seen with it on. Same thing with the case—like, the frame is bent.
We've run into this issue where people like my phone's not bent; it was in a case its whole life. It's like, well, you sat on the phone, you might have sat on the phone with the case on. You know, which you're like, your phone can still get bent inside of a case or cracked, for that matter. So we run into an operational issue there where they sent it in, they didn't know there was an issue with it. So, you know, a lot of... The devices that get sent into us, we end up re-quoting for some of those types of reasons, and so what we're trying to fight against is like— we feel the best thing for the customer is to like save their time on the front end of, like, check everything carefully, so that way you're not— you don't get a price that you're not wanting.
We have to send it back to you or anything like that. Got it. Okay. Thank you. Because our current process is basically like, it's like those first three same questions I just showed you, but then there's like the page with like six conditions. It's like, is your phone brand new, mint, good? You know, fair or faulty. And so they just pick that and there's like an explanation under them. But we're trying to get past that, mainly because people don't like to say their phone has issues. Like even on this question previously would be like, do you have any of the following issues? We're trying to reword so we're not saying the word 'issues' because people like generally think their phone's in better condition than it actually is.
Like, they're like, 'Oh, it's you know, it works perfect, it just has like a broken speaker, but like, yeah, it's a mint, it's mint condition, and great. It just like doesn't work, you know. Yeah. So you can't resell it and refurb it and give it to anyone. Okay. Makes sense. We'll still buy it. It's just that the customer is sending it as mint, and we're like, well, you know, it has the mic doesn't work or whatever. And they're like, 'Oh yeah,' but it's in great condition. So it should be worth more— type of thing. And that happens a lot, actually. Thank you. Um, and then. Same concept— most quickly get to the condition. If I answered yes to this question about sealed in the original box, that would give me the quote for a brand new device.
If I said no, it'd bring me here. And the idea behind this is you're just looking at the anywhere on the device. And if there's no scratches, dings, or dents, it would be mint. If there are scratches, dings, or dents, there's one final question, which is basically run the fingernail over the device. So if you can feel the scratches, it is fair. And if you can't, it's good. And so it's basically to reduce the complexity of people being like, well, I don't know the difference between good and fair. And this is one part I was definitely hoping to get your feedback on is what we have here is like your device's worth. This is obviously a page where we might lose a lot of people because they're not happy with the price or whatever.
We currently have it set up, and this is... That's how we have it set up now too, but you put in your email address and then click 'Get Paid'. So at least we're capturing the email for people so we can kind of follow up and chase them if they seemed interested in the device, but don't get through checkout. Okay. So that's the current idea behind that section. We used to not collect the email address and we just like lose them on checkout, but they'd have to get partway through checkout for us to ever get the email. Yeah, I think. Um, I think that's good. To get the email address. At least you're not making them wait to get the payout.
Because I think some will make you wait to get the value and send it to you via email. Which I think people would be annoyed with. So at least you're giving them the value. And if they like it, they can email you back. Um, about it or whatever, so I think that that works. Um, collecting the email address so you can send them the shipping label. Yeah. Okay. Thank you. Okay. And then this next page would be basically asking them if they have any other tech. And this is kind of the dropdown thing you asked about. So if it's answers, yes, it basically does that same process as before, but in dropdown form. Kind of like you mentioned. For the model and carrier.
So like you said, a good idea might be to switch that in the beginning to just this code instead. Hmm-hmm. The idea behind here is our most valuable customers are selling multiple devices. So it's basically to get as many devices as possible and like the easiest way possible as well, and to keep them on the checkout page. Currently, what this process looks like is you would just click 'Add Another Device' and it brings you back to the beginning. And the theory is that people are getting confused is like, 'Wait, did I just lose my whole order?' Because I'm back onto this original page I was previously on. So that's what this is trying to solve for, basically, is encouraging people: 'Hey, it's like easy right here to add a device.' So one option could be, and you're going to test it is: What if you only asked four questions, like three or four questions?
Without getting into the nitty gritty, and said, your device could be worth X, Y, Z. And then they finished the questionnaire to get the final. Um, only because. you have, if they're trying to get multiple devices, that could be a challenge. And so, um, you know, lock in your quote or whatever.

So I think some of those questions towards the end might have a higher bounce rate. Um, you'd have to look, use some like hotjar or true conversion or something and do some observational analytics and see, but I would see if maybe there's two or three. I think the main thing we're fighting against is the 'like whole bait and switch' thing where we're like, 'Here, here's your offer in good condition.' This is what it means. People don't really read through it because they don't. need to to get to the next step so, just like click 'Mint,' go to the next step. Then we get the phone. We're like, 'Hey, this phone's in fair condition; it's like very scratched up.' It aligns with what we said on our site.
It just like wasn't read through at the time of sending it. So, the happy medium between, like, we can get a good conversion rate, like with our current process, right? But then we're ending up changing the price once we get it. Which obviously the customer is not happy about. And we'll send it back free of charge, but it wastes their time. So we're trying to find a happy medium between like people actually being willing to send it to us in the first place and not having to re-quote them. Right. Right.
